examples.title: NFA Simulation for Pattern Matching
examples.description: Simulating the NFA constructed from a pattern on input text. Tracks all possible active states at each position.
examples.explanation: This NFA simulation maintains a set of all possible states the automaton could be in after reading each character. When the accepting state (representing complete pattern match) is in the active set, a match ending at current position is recorded. Time complexity: O(mn) in worst case due to multiple active states.

examples.title: DFA Construction for Pattern Matching
examples.description: Constructs the deterministic finite automaton transition table from a pattern using the standard algorithm.
examples.explanation: The key insight is that each state represents the length of the longest prefix of pattern that matches a suffix of the processed input. When reading symbol a in state q, we find the longest prefix of pattern that is a suffix of (current matched prefix + a). This ensures the automaton always knows how much of the pattern is currently matched.

examples.title: DFA-based Text Search
examples.description: Simulating the DFA on input text to find all pattern occurrences in O(n) time.
examples.code:
function DFA_SEARCH(pattern P[1..m], text T[1..n]):
  DFA = BUILD_DFA(P, alphabet(T))
  state = 0  // Start state
  matches = []
  
  for i from 1 to n:
    symbol = T[i]
    state = DFA[state][symbol]  // Deterministic transition
    
    if state == m:  // Accepting state reached
      // Pattern ends at position i
      match_start = i - m + 1
      add match_start to matches
  
  return matches
examples.explanation: This is the core DFA-based pattern search algorithm. Each character of the text causes exactly one table lookup and state transition, giving O(n) time complexity. The match is reported when the automaton reaches state m (the accepting state). No backtracking or reprocessing of text characters ever occurs.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m|Σ|) for DFA table + O(1) for algorithm
